evidence_quotes.quote: Providers of online payment services must not affect the products/services sold by the entity, which should only be subject to the applicable law of the legality of the trade in the good.

A purchased digital good (such as software/applications/games/films/music) cannot be permanently removed or accessed from existence merely because the manufacturer has decided, for subjective reasons, to stop maintaining the server infrastructure required for the operation of the good and thereby completely blocking access to the purchased good.

paragraphs.text: In my view, the law should extend its scope and cover two cases: 1. Providers of online payment services must not affect the products/services sold by the entity, which should only be subject to the applicable law of the legality of the trade in the good. A drafting example could be: Company ‘V’ presses another company, e.g. ‘S’, to remove otherwise legitimate products from the sale only because (subjectively) their sale may negatively affect the image of ‘V’, threatening to cut off the online payment services managed by the duopoly and cartel of ‘V’ and ‘MC’, thereby (in practice) disconnecting any online sales. 2. A purchased digital good (such as software/applications/games/films/music) cannot be permanently removed or accessed from existence merely because the manufacturer has decided, for subjective reasons, to stop maintaining the server infrastructure required for the operation of the good and thereby completely blocking access to the purchased good. The legal minimum should ensure the establishment of an ‘end of life plan’ and the implementation of solutions to make it possible to use the good itself and at its own risk. Negative examples of this problem can be: Company ‘M’ excludes an app to buy films on which people bought films and, with its exclusion, lose irretrievably access to their library. Company ‘A’ sold the graphical editing software and decides to switch off the servers supporting its operation and thus users are forced to purchase a new version or lose the tool. ‘U’ sold the game and, one day, switched off the servers required for the game, thereby irretrievably withdrawing access to the goods purchased to the holders, servers which could be hosted by interested holders of the game in question or maintained by them at external sub-suppliers of cloud services if such an option had been provided in the termination plan.
